archetype: Real-Time Haptic-Feedback Haptic-Force Cancellation Mesh for Robotic Tele-Operated Arterial Suturing
description: A distributed control system that synchronizes master-slave robotic arms across satellite links to provide haptic force-feedback while actively canceling transmission latency-induced jitter during arterial anastomosis. It is used by vascular surgeons operating remotely from high-stakes environments, requiring nanosecond-level determinism to prevent tissue damage. The technical difficulty lies in bridging the gap between human proprioception and network physics, ensuring the surgeon feels the suture tension in real-time despite 200ms+ round-trip delays.
rules: MUST implement IEEE 11073 SDC (Service Oriented Device Connectivity) for real-time medical device data exchange with sub-millisecond latency guarantees.

MUST utilize deterministic networking protocols (e.g., IEEE 802.1Qbv Time-Sensitive Networking) to enforce strict time slots for haptic data packets, preventing jitter from disrupting force feedback loops.
MUST integrate a predictive Kalman filter model trained on specific arterial tissue viscoelasticity data to extrapolate tissue position during network blackouts without overshooting.
MUST comply with IEC 62304 for medical device software lifecycle management, ensuring every state transition is logged for forensic auditability in case of surgical error.
MUST enforce a 'Kill-Switch' logic that immediately disengages the slave robot if the force-feedback discrepancy exceeds 0.5 Newtons, overriding any software prediction algorithm.
anti_patterns: DO NOT use standard TCP/IP for haptic data transmission; packet reordering or retransmission delays will cause 'phantom force' oscillations that can tear delicate artery walls.
DO NOT rely on cloud-based AI for real-time trajectory prediction; the inference latency variance is unacceptable for loop-closure control in surgical settings.
DO NOT implement 'best-effort' quality of service; the system must guarantee bounded latency, as even a 10ms spike can result in the suture cutting through the vessel.
key_technical_challenges: Designing a latency-compensation algorithm that distinguishes between network lag and actual tissue resistance to prevent 'ghost vibrations' in the haptic controller.
Synchronizing multi-modal sensor fusion (tactile, visual, acoustic) across geographically distributed nodes where clock drift exceeds 1000x the control loop frequency.
Achieving deterministic hardware interrupt handling within 20 microseconds to ensure the master-slave kinematic mapping remains within safe force limits under high load.

recommended_stack.language: Rust (Memory safety without garbage collection pauses is critical for real-time control loops)
recommended_stack.framework: ROS 2 with RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe) for deterministic, middleware-agnostic hardware communication
recommended_stack.database_engine: TimescaleDB (High-write throughput for continuous sensor streams with precise time-series querying for forensic analysis)
recommended_stack.cache: null (In-memory shared memory rings are required to avoid serialization overhead; traditional caching layers introduce non-deterministic latency)
recommended_stack.message_broker: ZeroMQ (Configured for pre-allocated sockets and Dgram sockets for ultra-low latency, non-blocking I/O)
special_services: Network Delay Jitter Predictor and Pre-Emptive Compensator
